prompt: Se fossi uno user che installa questa skill per la prima volta, cosa ti confonderebbe nei primi 5 minuti?
content:
name split: repo is SIGIL, public brand is Hewn, commands half-named flint — `hewn`, `hewn-ir`, but slash commands `/flint`, `/flint-on`, `/flint-audit`, and wire header `@flint v0 hybrid`. user asks "what do I type."

four install artifacts dumped at once: slash skills, output-styles (four of them: hewn / hewn-thinking / flint / flint-thinking), `hewn` binary, `hewn-ir` Python CLI. README explains each in one sentence each, not when to reach for which.

`hewn` vs `hewn-ir` confusable by one char. one wraps claude, other parses IR. memory already flags people conflate them.

`hewn` vs `hewn-mcp` vs `/flint` slash skill — three doors to "turn on Flint", trade-offs buried mid-README.

install URL still `tommy29tmar/flint` after rebrand. user pastes one-liner, sees old name, wonders if wrong repo.

"thinking-mode" term shows up in headline ("Hewn thinking-mode at system-prompt level") without defining what thinking-mode is or how it differs from the slash skill.

IR format subtle: call form `f("x")` vs suffix `f_"x"` — first Flint output user writes by hand will almost certainly use forbidden suffix form and not know why parser rejects.

`/flint-audit` needs a file or paste but skill description doesn't show an example invocation; user stares at prompt.

output-style vs skill vs binary mental model never drawn — user doesn't know "output-style loads as context, can't override Claude Code system prompt, that's why always-on via style alone is unreliable" is the whole reason `hewn` binary exists. that paragraph is the keystone and it's paragraph 6.
